steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: "The part of a compound that determines the compound's color is [blank] the chromophore." The verb "is" and its complement "the chromophore" must stay connected. The only thing that belongs in the blank is the word that completes the predicate — no punctuation should interrupt it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘called-’: a dash after "called" breaks the verb phrase from its complement, leaving "the chromophore" as a dangling fragment. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘called;’: a semicolon separates two independent clauses, but "the chromophore" is not a clause — it is a bare noun complement. A semicolon cannot precede it. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘called.’: a period ends the sentence, turning "the chromophore" into a one-word fragment with no verb. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘called’: no punctuation allows "is called the chromophore" to read as one complete predicate. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
